title: Japanese Chicken Katsu Curry

ingredients.name: boneless chicken breasts or thighs (2 large pieces)

subs.name: thick slices of paneer or firm tofu, coated and fried the same way, for a vegetarian katsu curry

ingredients.name: maida (plain flour), for coating and roux
visual_checks: about 50 g for coating, 30 g for the roux
ingredients.notes: about 50 g for coating, 30 g for the roux

ingredients.name: panko breadcrumbs or fine dried breadcrumbs
visual_checks: panko gives the lightest crust; regular breadcrumbs also work
ingredients.notes: panko gives the lightest crust; regular breadcrumbs also work

subs.name: finely crushed cornflakes for a lighter, gluten-free-ish crust

ingredients.name: cooking oil (for frying)
visual_checks: for shallow or deep frying; use less for shallow frying
ingredients.notes: for shallow or deep frying; use less for shallow frying

ingredients.name: potato, peeled and cut into 3 cm chunks

subs.name: 2 tbsp oil for the roux — the curry will be slightly less rich but otherwise identical

ingredients.name: curry powder (mild Japanese-style or any mild blend)
visual_checks: S&B brand if available; or use a mild homemade blend of cumin, coriander and turmeric
ingredients.notes: S&B brand if available; or use a mild homemade blend of cumin, coriander and turmeric

subs.name: equal parts cumin powder, coriander powder, turmeric, with a pinch of cardamom and clove for a DIY Japanese-style spice blend
subs.notes: milder and less heat-forward than standard Indian curry powder

ingredients.name: soy sauce
visual_checks: adds umami depth to the curry
ingredients.notes: adds umami depth to the curry

steps.text: Pound each chicken piece to an even 1 cm thickness using a rolling pin or meat mallet — this ensures even cooking when frying. Season both sides with salt.

Set up three shallow bowls: one with flour, one with beaten egg, one with breadcrumbs. Coat each chicken piece first in flour (shake off excess), then dip completely in egg, then press firmly into breadcrumbs on both sides. Let the coated pieces rest on a plate for 5 minutes so the coating adheres.

Meanwhile start the curry: heat 1 tbsp butter in a heavy-bottomed pot over medium flame. Add onions and cook for 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until soft and golden. Add potato and carrot and stir for 2 minutes to coat in the butter.

Push the vegetables to one side of the pot. Add the remaining 2 tbsp butter to the empty space, let it melt, then stir in the flour. Cook the butter-flour paste for 1 minute until it turns a faint biscuit colour, then add the curry powder and fry for 30 seconds until fragrant.

Pour in the stock and soy sauce gradually, stirring constantly to incorporate the roux without lumps. Bring to a simmer, cover and cook for 15 minutes until the potato and carrot are completely tender. The curry should coat a spoon thickly. Adjust salt.

Shallow-fry the katsu: pour about 2 cm of oil into a kadai or frying pan over medium-high heat. When a breadcrumb sizzles on contact, add the chicken and fry for 4–5 minutes per side until deep golden and cooked through. Drain on kitchen paper.

Slice the katsu into 2 cm strips. Plate a mound of steamed rice, lean katsu slices against it, and spoon the curry generously over everything. Serve immediately — the coating loses its crunch if it sits in the curry for long.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: chicken → thick slices of paneer or firm tofu, coated and fried the same way, for a vegetarian katsu curry; bread → finely crushed cornflakes for a lighter, gluten-free-ish crust (also changes gluten allergen profile); butter → 2 tbsp oil for the roux — the curry will be slightly less rich but otherwise identical (removes dairy allergen); curry-powder → equal parts cumin powder, coriander powder, turmeric, with a pinch of cardamom and clove for a DIY Japanese-style spice blend (milder and less heat-forward than standard Indian curry powder)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Fry the katsu in a kadai with 2–3 cm of oil over medium-high heat — it works exactly like making cutlets. Japanese S&B curry powder is available at specialty stores; a mild DIY blend of 1 tsp each cumin, coriander, turmeric plus a pinch of cardamom works well. The roux step is essentially white sauce (béchamel) technique — standard in any Indian kitchen that makes pasta bake or bread rolls.
